name: demons threatening the deceased
shortDescription: Various demons in Egyptian mythology threatened the deceased during their journey through the underworld, particularly during the Weighing of the Heart ceremony.
description: In Egyptian mythology, the afterlife was fraught with peril, and the deceased faced numerous challenges in their journey to the Field of Reeds. Among the most significant threats were various demons and monstrous beings who sought to prevent the unworthy from achieving eternal life. These demons often lurked in the Duat, the underworld, and posed a danger particularly during the Weighing of the Heart ceremony. During this crucial judgment, the deceased's heart was weighed against the feather of Ma'at (truth and justice). Demons such as Ammit ('the Devourer'), with the head of a crocodile, the body of a lion, and the hindquarters of a hippopotamus, stood ready to devour the hearts of those deemed impure, thus condemning them to non-existence. Other demons, often depicted with animal heads and wielding knives or other weapons, attempted to disrupt the weighing process or attack the deceased directly. Spells and incantations from the Book of the Dead were essential for the deceased to navigate the underworld, ward off these demons, and pass the tests necessary to achieve eternal life. The specific names and descriptions of these demons varied, but their purpose remained constant: to act as guardians and punishers, ensuring that only the righteous could enter the afterlife.

sources.title: The Egyptian Book of the Dead
sources.author: Various Scribes

sources.title: Ancient Egyptian Literature: A Book of Readings
sources.author: Miriam Lichtheim
